title: Paris-Brest
subtitle: Choux ring filled with hazelnut praline cream
excerpt: A large choux ring piped into a circle, baked until hollow and deep gold, split and filled with a rich hazelnut praline cream. A French patisserie classic. Serves 8.
sourceNotes: The Paris-Brest was created in 1910 by pastry chef Louis Durand to celebrate a Paris–Brest–Paris bicycle race, the ring shape representing a wheel. It is documented in French patisserie guides from the early twentieth century and appears in Auguste Escoffier's later editions. The hazelnut praline cream filling, a mousseline cream blended with praline paste, is the standard form recorded in twentieth-century French household patisserie books.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The choux ring can be baked a day ahead and stored in a tin. Fill on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 210°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A praline cream for Paris-Brest is made by blending hazelnut praline paste into a crème mousseline — a crème pâtissière beaten with soft butter. The praline paste is made by caramelising sugar with the hazelnuts, letting it set on oiled paper, then blending until it forms a smooth, oily paste. The mousseline must be at room temperature when you beat in the butter — too cold and it will curdle; too warm and it will be loose. Beat the softened butter in gradually until the cream is thick, glossy, and holds its shape when piped.

content.text: This is a three-component pastry: choux ring, hazelnut praline paste, and the cream. The ring bakes the same as profiteroles — deep gold, hollow. The
is the most technically demanding step. Make it cold: the cream must be fully cooled before you beat in the butter or it will collapse into a greasy mess.

Toast the {{hazelnuts}} at 180°C fan for 10 minutes until golden. Rub off the skins in a cloth. Melt the {{caster-sugar}} (praline amount) in a dry saucepan over medium heat until deep amber. Stir in the hazelnuts and tip onto oiled baking paper. Leave to set hard. Break into pieces and blend in a food processor until it forms a smooth paste — this takes 3–5 minutes. Set aside.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} (ring amount) with {{sea-salt-fine}} and 240 ml water. Bring to a boil. Remove from heat, beat in the {{plain-flour}} (ring amount) all at once, dry over heat for 1 minute. Cool 5 minutes. Beat in {{eggs}} (ring amount) gradually until the paste is glossy and falls in a slow ribbon. Draw a 22 cm circle on baking paper. Pipe two concentric choux rings on the circle, then a third ring on top of the join. Scatter with flaked {{blanched-almonds}}. Bake 35–40 minutes, deep gold. Pierce and dry for 5 minutes in the off oven. Cool completely.
Make the praline cream and fill

Heat the {{whole-milk}} to a simmer. Whisk the {{caster-sugar}} (cream amount) with the {{eggs}} (cream yolks) and {{plain-flour}} (cream amount). Pour the hot milk over while whisking, return to the pan, and cook over medium heat until thick and the flour is cooked out — about 3 minutes. Press baking paper against the surface and chill until cold. Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} (cream amount) until pale. Beat in the cold pastry cream a tablespoon at a time until smooth. Beat in the praline paste. Pipe generously into the split choux ring. Replace the lid, dust with {{icing-sugar}}, and chill for 1 hour before serving.
